narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 35, upper-middle family income background, part-time enrollment plans, and school deciles placing you in the 1st decile for Year 1 grades and 2nd for Year 3—details that paint a picture of your academic journey so far. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 40% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is high enough to tip the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's bar passage rates. This prediction wasn't random; it zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that carried the most weight in its decision. Your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive force, pushing the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass because it's solid, though slightly below the typical student's 36.7 average. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile ranking had the biggest negative pull, nudging the prediction toward failure since being in the 2nd decile—well below the average 5th or 6th—signals weaker performance relative to peers at a similar stage. Your upper-middle family income also helped your case somewhat, as it aligns with backgrounds that often correlate with better outcomes, countering some of the concerns. Compared to typical students, who are mostly full-time and boast higher GPAs around 3.21, your part-time path and earlier strong Year 1 decile offered some balance but couldn't fully offset the Year 3 dip. While your profile shows real potential, especially in that LSAT strength, the model weighed these factors as leaning too risky overall for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your character or determination—many students like you overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or extra courses, and exploring programs that value part-time students or offer bar prep support. You might also reach out to admissions for feedback or apply to schools with holistic reviews that highlight your unique Hispanic perspective and resilience. With focused improvements, a stronger prediction is within reach next time.
